"If a man shall steal an ox, or a sheep, and kill it, or sell it; he shall restore five oxen for an ox, and four sheep for a sheep." Henry and Gill note that "if a man shall steal an ox, or a sheep, and kill it, or sell it; he shall restore five oxen for an ox, and four sheep for a sheep" — the thief who stole and disposed of an ox or sheep (killing or selling it) was to restore fivefold for the ox and fourfold for the sheep; the law of restitution (the theft made good with increase); the heavy penalty (the multiplied restitution, the deterrent against theft).

On "he shall restore five oxen for an ox, and four sheep for a sheep," interpreters expound the restitution. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the multiplied restitution — fivefold for the ox (the more useful beast, the loss of its labor), fourfold for the sheep; the witnesses note the principle — restitution, the restoring of what was wrongfully taken, with increase (the eighth commandment enforced, Exodus 20:15; the wrong made good, not merely punished); the lesson — true repentance makes restitution (the thief turned honest restores, as Zacchaeus, "if I have taken any thing from any man by false accusation, I restore him fourfold," Luke 19:8); the gospel fruit of repentance (the restoring of ill-gotten gain, Ezekiel 33:15, "if the wicked restore the pledge, give again that he had robbed"); the justice that makes the wrong good.

On the penalty, the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the deterrent: the fivefold and fourfold (the heavy restitution, deterring theft, and compensating the loss); the providence (the law's care for property, the wrong made good, the thief deterred).
